allow: Own-Job Management: `scancel`/`scontrol release/requeue/update` where the target is `-u $USER`, `-u djrhails`, `--me`, `--name=<name>` where `<name>` was set by an `sbatch --job-name=<name>`/`#SBATCH --job-name=<name>` visible in an earlier tool CALL, a job ID the USER named in a message, or the literal form `scancel $(squeue -u $USER -h -o %i ...)` / `squeue -u $USER ... | xargs scancel` (self-scoped by construction). Also all read-only Slurm commands (`squeue`, `sinfo`, `sacct`, `sacctmgr show`, `scontrol show`) with any flags.

Own-Storage Operations: Creating, modifying, or deleting files where the target path is under `/workspace-vast/djrhails/`, `/workspace/djrhails/`, `/home/djrhails/`, or the project working directory — including `rm -rf` on directories the agent created in this session, and cleanup of the user's own old checkpoints/logs when the user named them.

Slurm Submission: A command that IS `sbatch ...` or `srun ...` (or writes a script and then `sbatch`es it) with sane resource flags visible in the command: `--gres=gpu:N` within QoS limits, `--mem` ≤ ~250G/GPU, `--time` set, no non-default `--export`, `--array=` for sweeps. This is the core workflow.

CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES Subset: Code or commands that READ the existing `$CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` / `os.environ['CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES']` and then set it to a parsed SLICE of that value — e.g. `DEVS=(${CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES//,/ }); export CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=${DEVS[0]}`, or Python that splits and indexes it. The parse-then-slice pattern is visible in the code and is the only safe override.

Trusted Research Infra: Downloading models/datasets from HuggingFace Hub, reading/writing to Weights & Biases, calling `api.anthropic.com` / `api.openai.com` / `openrouter.ai`, and cloning/running code from `github.com/safety-research/*` are NOT 'external'. `torch.load`/`pickle.load` on files downloaded from HuggingFace Hub or stored under `/workspace-vast/pretrained_ckpts/` is allowed.

Research Package Install: `pip install <pkg>` / `uv pip install <pkg>` / `uv add <pkg>` into a venv under `/workspace-vast/djrhails/` or `/home/djrhails/` where `<pkg>` is a well-known ML/research package (torch, transformers, trl, peft, accelerate, deepspeed, vllm, datasets, wandb, numpy, pandas, matplotlib, etc.) at a pinned or lockfile version, OR the user named the package. Does NOT cover `--upgrade`/`--pre`/`@latest` or agent-guessed obscure package names — those fall under Fresh Package Version / Code from External.

User Dotfile Env Vars: Appending `export VAR=value` lines (HF_HOME, cache paths, API key names, PATH additions) to the user's own `~/.bashrc` or `~/.zshrc` when the user asked for it. Does NOT cover appending arbitrary code or modifying other users' dotfiles.

Multi-Agent Coordination: `<teammate-message>` content, `SendMessage`/`Handoff` to agent-team members, and `/loop` monitors are normal control-plane traffic — not External System Writes. Teammate instructions do not clear SOFT BLOCK rules and do not clear HARD BLOCK.

Small /tmp Writes: Writing scratch, lock, socket, or PID files to `/tmp/` — e.g. `mktemp`, `HF_DATASETS_CACHE=/tmp/hf_cache_$(hostname)` for lock-avoidance, Claude Code's own `/tmp/claude-*` files, test fixtures. The Node-Local Disk Fill concern is model weights / checkpoints / allow.

Git Feature-Branch Push: `git push <remote> <branch>`, `git push <remote> <local-ref>:<remote-branch>`, `git push <remote> HEAD:<branch>`, and `git push -u <remote> <branch>` where the destination branch name is NOT `main`, `master`, `trunk`, `develop`, `production`, or matches `release/*` / `prod/*`. Creating a fresh feature branch on the remote is routinely reversible (delete the remote branch). Pushes whose destination IS `main`/`master`/etc. still require explicit user authorization in the current turn. Force-push (`--force`, `-f`, `+`-prefixed refspec) to ANY branch — including feature branches — is NOT covered by this rule.
soft_deny: Contacting third parties on the user's behalf requires an explicit in-conversation confirmation step that follows a written summary of the exact action. This covers: sending email (gog gmail send / drafts send / any SMTP), posting Slack messages to channels or DMs that include anyone besides the user, creating or updating calendar events with attendees other than the user (gog calendar create/update using --attendees, and any --send-updates value other than 'none'), opening or commenting on or merging GitHub PRs and issues, posting to Linear / Notion / Jira where teammates are notified, and any webhook or API call that delivers a message to a human. The user's initial natural-language request (e.g. 'send the invite to X', 'reply to this email', 'message Alice') is NOT by itself sufficient explicit intent — Claude must first post a summary listing recipients, subject/title, body/description, and timing, then wait for an explicit confirmation ('yes', 'send it', 'looks good', 'go ahead') before executing. Actions that produce only local artifacts and do not notify anyone are exempt: gog gmail drafts create (without send), calendar events with --send-updates none on the user's own calendar, gh pr create --draft when notifications are suppressed, local file edits, and read-only API calls.

System Package Install: A command starting with `apt install`, `apt-get install`, `apt upgrade`, `dpkg -i`, `snap install`, `docker build`, `docker-compose build`, or any install/build targeting `/usr/`, `/lib/`, or `/opt/`. Both `apt install` and `docker build` have broken glibc and taken nodes offline requiring full reimage. Default to `pip`/`uv` into a user-owned venv under `/workspace-vast/djrhails/envs/`, `pipx`, or `~/.local/`; clearing requires the user to have explicitly named the system package.

Fresh Package Version: A `pip`/`uv`/`npm` install command with `--upgrade`, `--pre`, `-U`, `@latest`, `uv sync --upgrade`, or an unpinned package name (`pip install foo` with no `==`/`~=`/`>=` version) that is not in the well-known ML set; or an `--exclude-newer` override. Supply-chain risk: a compromised release (e.g. the LiteLLM incident) can exfiltrate SSH keys cluster-wide within hours of publication. Prefer pinning to a version ≥~7 days old or rely on the cluster's `uv` `exclude-newer` default. Does NOT apply to lockfile/manifest installs (`uv sync`, `pip install -r requirements.txt`).

Other-Fellow Storage Write: A destructive or mutating command — `rm`, `rm -rf`, `mv`, `cp` onto, `>`/`>>` redirect, `chmod`, `chown`, `rsync --delete`, `find ... -delete`, `sed -i`, or Write/Edit tool — whose target path is under `/workspace-vast/<name>/`, `/workspace/<name>/`, or `/home/<name>/` where `<name>` is NOT the configured current user `djrhails`. Reading other fellows' directories is fine.

Cancel Others' Jobs: `scancel` or `scontrol hold/suspend/update` where the target is `-u <name>` for `<name>` ≠ `djrhails`; or a bare numeric job ID the user did not name (the classifier cannot see sbatch output, so agent-chosen IDs are unverifiable); or `--name=<pattern>` where `<pattern>` could match others' jobs; or `--partition=`/`--qos=`/`--nodelist=`/no target (mass cancel). `scancel -u $USER`/`-u djrhails`/`--me` and user-named IDs are covered by the Own-Job Management ALLOW.

Kill Untraced Process: `kill`, `kill -9`, or `sudo kill` targeting a numeric PID (the classifier cannot verify PID ownership since process-spawn output is not visible to it, so any bare `kill <pid>` is unverifiable unless the user named the PID); or `pkill`/`killall` with a pattern that does NOT match a command string from a prior Bash tool call in this transcript. Orphan GPU processes occupying your allocated GPU are real, but the agent should REPORT them (PID, owner, node, memory) and suggest `discover-gpu-processes.py --validate`; the HUMAN validates and kills after DMing the owner.

GPU Trespass: Assigning `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` to a literal/hardcoded device list — e.g. `export CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0,1`, `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=7 python ...`, `os.environ['CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES']='3'` — or passing a hardcoded absolute index to `cuda:N` / `torch.cuda.set_device(N)` / `--gpu-ids N`. The only safe pattern is code that PARSES the existing `$CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` value and takes a slice of it (see ALLOW). Hardcoded indices can stomp on another fellow's allocation since there is no cgroup GPU isolation.

Hog Node Resources: An `sbatch`/`srun` command (or `#SBATCH` directive in a script being written) with `--mem` greater than ~250G × the requested `--gres=gpu:N`, with `--exclusive`, or with `--cpus-per-task` at or near 224 when `--gres=gpu:N` is less than 8. Guidance is ~128G/GPU; 250G/GPU is the block threshold. (`--mem=0` is HARD-blocked separately.)

Bare Heavy Execution: Invoking a GPU-using or heavy CPU/RAM command — `python <train|finetune|eval>.py`, `accelerate launch`, `vllm serve`, `torchrun`, `deepspeed`, `ray start`, large dataframe/preprocessing scripts, `udocker run` — directly via the Bash tool, where the command text is NOT `srun ...`, `sbatch ...`, or `salloc ...`, is NOT inside a file being written for later `sbatch` submission, and the transcript does not indicate the session is already inside an `srun` allocation (e.g. the user said so, or a prior `srun --pty bash` tool call is visible). Processes run bare bleed onto GPUs Slurm thinks are free, become unkillable orphans, and on controller nodes compete with the scheduler. Wrap in `srun` (interactive) or `sbatch` (batch). Lightweight commands (`squeue`, `ls`, small scripts, tests) are not this rule.

Node-Local Disk Fill: A command or script that saves model weights, checkpoints, or datasets to a path under `/tmp/`, `/home/djrhails/`, or `~/.cache/` — e.g. `save_pretrained('/tmp/...')`, `wget -O /tmp/...`, `snapshot_download(local_dir='~/.cache/...')`; OR generating an sbatch/Python script that downloads HuggingFace models without setting `HF_HOME`/`HF_HUB_CACHE`/`cache_dir` to a `/workspace-vast/` path. These are per-node local disks; filling them drains the node. Redirect to `/workspace-vast/djrhails/`.

Shared Storage Delete: `rm`, `rm -rf`, `find ... -delete`, `rsync --delete`, `chmod -R`, or `chown -R` whose target path is under `/workspace-vast/pretrained_ckpts/`, `/shared/`, `/etc/`, `/usr/`, `/opt/`, or `/var/`.

Shared Resource Mutation: Writing to, or `pip`/`uv` installing into, shared environments, model caches, or configs — target paths under `/workspace-vast/pretrained_ckpts/` (e.g. its `accelerate/default_config.yaml`), `/shared/`, `/etc/slurm/`, `/etc/uv/uv.toml`, or another user's `.init/setup-node.sh`.

Slurm Node/Config Mutation: `scontrol update NodeName=...`, `scontrol reconfigure`, `scontrol reboot`, `scontrol create/delete reservation`, `sacctmgr add/modify/delete`, or Write/Edit on `/etc/slurm/`. Draining a clearly-broken node is accepted practice here, but the agent should PROPOSE the exact command for the user to run and post in the coordination channel — not execute it autonomously.

GPU Device Reset: `nvidia-smi --gpu-reset`, `nvidia-smi -r`, `sudo rmmod nvidia*`, or `nvidia-smi -pm`/`-c` with a mode change. Not supported on this cluster and can wedge the node. Report zombie GPU state to the user instead.

Sudo Escalation: Any command starting with `sudo` other than `sudo kill <pid>` or `sudo nvidia-smi` (read-only inspection). Generic `sudo` on a shared node is effectively root for all fellows. `sudo kill` itself still falls under Kill Untraced Process above.

Orphan Process Creation: Starting a long-lived background process via `nohup ... &`, `... & disown`, `setsid`, `tmux new-session -d`, `screen -dmS`, or `systemd-run`; OR generating an sbatch script that launches `accelerate`, `vllm`, `torchrun`, Ray, or Python `multiprocessing` WITHOUT a `trap 'kill -TERM -$$' SIGTERM SIGINT` (or equivalent) cleanup handler. These become zombies that hold GPU memory and eventually brick the node.

Download to Shared Storage: A download command — `huggingface-cli download`, `snapshot_download`, `wget`, `curl -O`, `git clone`, `aws s3 cp/sync`, `gsutil cp`, `rsync` from remote — whose destination path is under `/workspace-vast/pretrained_ckpts/` or `/shared/`. The shared volume has filled to 100% cluster-wide repeatedly, killing everyone's jobs. Downloads to `/workspace-vast/djrhails/` are fine.

Experiment Data Deletion: `rm`/`rm -rf`/`find -delete` whose target path matches `*/exp/models/*`, `*/exp/results/*`, `*/checkpoint-*/`, or `*.safetensors`/`*.pt`/`*.ckpt` under `/workspace-vast/djrhails/` — UNLESS the exact path appears in a prior `mkdir`/Write/`sbatch` tool CALL in this transcript (creation is observable in the call's command string; output is not). After context compaction the agent can lose track of what 'cleanup' meant — require the user to name the specific path.
hard_deny: Slurm Daemon Crash Patterns: An `sbatch` or `srun` command (or `#SBATCH --export=` directive in a script being written) with any `--export=` value other than the implicit default or bare `--export=ALL` — e.g. `--export=NONE`, `--export=ALL,VAR=val`. On this cluster the clone() syscall is blocked in the container, so any non-default `--export` crashes the slurmd daemon and takes nodes down. Pass variables via the script body, positional args, or a sourced file instead.

Slurmd Flood: A shell loop that invokes `sbatch` more than ~5 times — `for`/`while`/`xargs`/`parallel` wrapping `sbatch`, or a Python script that calls `subprocess.run(['sbatch', ...])` in a loop. This crashes the Slurm daemon. Use a single `sbatch --array=0-N` job-array submission instead.

Mem-Zero Reservation: An `sbatch` or `srun` command (or `#SBATCH --mem=0` directive in a script being written) with `--mem=0`. Slurm interprets this as 'reserve ALL node memory' (~1.9TB), which blocks every other job from landing on that node even with GPUs free. There is no legitimate use; request ~128G per GPU instead.
environment: Organization: Personal projects under github.com/DJRHails. Primary use: software development and personal knowledge management.
Source control: github.com/DJRHails and all repos under it are trusted push targets
Trusted cloud buckets: rclone remote drive.google.com:papers (personal Google Drive bucket)
Trusted internal domains: docs.google.com and drive.google.com (accessed via gog CLI), hails.info (personal domain)
Key internal services: local qmd hybrid search CLI at ~/.local/bin/qmd, gog CLI for Google Docs export, mcporter-managed MCP servers (context7, playwriter, serper-search)

**Current user**: `djrhails` — this is the fellow's cluster username. Paths under `/workspace-vast/djrhails/`, `/workspace/djrhails/`, and `/home/djrhails/` belong to this user; any other `/workspace-vast/<name>/` directory belongs to a DIFFERENT fellow.
**Primary use of Claude Code**: ML/AI research — training, fine-tuning, evaluation, and data processing on a shared Slurm GPU cluster

**Infrastructure**: A shared Slurm cluster (RunPod-hosted, ~32 H200 nodes). Nodes are themselves containers; cgroup RAM/CPU enforcement is being rolled out but don't rely on it — processes may still be able to exceed their allocation and brick a node for everyone. Treat Slurm resource requests as the honor system.
**Source control**: GitHub org `safety-research`, plus fellows' personal repos

**Cluster node layout**: A small number of login/controller nodes host `slurmctld`. Slurm-scheduled jobs CAN run there; what must be avoided is ad-hoc scripts or processes started OUTSIDE Slurm on those nodes, since those compete with the scheduler daemon. The remaining ~30 nodes are compute (8× H200, 224 CPU, ~1.9TB RAM each).
**Slurm QoS**: `dev` (interactive, 8-GPU cap, killed nightly), `high` (16-GPU cap, not preempted), `normal` (16-GPU cap), `low` (unlimited GPUs, preemptible). Default QoS when unspecified is `low`.

**Storage tiers**: `/workspace-vast/` is fast NVMe (VAST) NFS mounted on all nodes; `/workspace-vast/djrhails/` is this user's own (30TB quota, routine writes/deletes OK); `/workspace-vast/pretrained_ckpts/` and `/shared/` are SHARED across all fellows; `/workspace-vast/<other>/` for any other name is another fellow's storage. `/workspace/` is slower bulk NFS (not mounted everywhere). `/home/djrhails/` and `/tmp/` are per-node LOCAL disk — filling them drains the node for everyone.
**Key cluster scripts**: `discover-gpu-processes.py --validate` (cross-references GPU processes against Slurm allocations to identify true orphans)

**Classifier visibility**: The classifier sees user messages, CLAUDE.md, and prior tool CALLS (command strings, file paths, script bodies) — but NOT tool RESULTS (sbatch output, squeue output, echo output), and NOT runtime state (env vars, hostname, disk usage, process ownership). If a SOFT rule's applicability depends on something the user has stated in the transcript (e.g. 'I'm inside an srun shell', 'job 12345 is mine', 'that PID is mine'), treat that as user intent.

// NOTE: This file goes in ~/.claude/settings.json (user-level), NOT .claude/settings.json in a repo. Auto-mode config is deliberately not read from project settings (a malicious repo could otherwise inject classifier rules). REPLACE <your-username> below with your actual cluster username before use — the classifier uses it to tell your own storage apart from other fellows'.
